t: Severity assessment (initial PANSS or BPRS score).
Toxicology screening (urine) and substance use history.
An affective episode is present during a significant portion of the psychotic symptoms, but psychosis also persists during periods outside the affective episode.
Psychotic-like symptoms; full psychosis criteria absent; persistent personality traits.
Only delirium, hallucination minimal or none; function relatively preserved.
Psychosis exists only in the context of manic or depressive episodes.
Temporal relationship with substance use or withdrawal; remission after substance elimination.
Medical condition-related (epilepsy, autoimmune encephalitis, brain tumor, Wilson's disease).
Early onset, social-communicative deficit primary; hallucinations/delusions not typical.
Personality disorders (6D10) — with schizotypal and paranoid characteristics
Persistent personality traits, no full psychotic episodes.
SCID-5 (for DSM-5), MINI (short, accessible in clinical practice).
PANSS (30 indicators, 7-point scale), BPRS (18 indicators), SANS/SAPS (negative/positive separately), CGI-S.
GAF (DSM-IV), PSP (Personal and Social Performance Scale).
MATRICS Consensus Cognitive Battery (MCCB) — research and specialized clinics.

Complete blood count, liver and kidney function tests, fasting glucose and HbA1c, lipid profile, TSH and T4, vitamin B12 and folate, toxicology screening (cannabis, amphetamine, opioid, cocaine, PCP, MDMA), HIV and syphilis serology, pregnancy test (in females), baseline prolactin level.

EEG (rule out seizure or delirium), anti-NMDA-R antibodies (CSF — atypical presentation), ceruloplasmin and 24-hour urine copper (suspected Wilson's disease), B12/folate/homocysteine (suspected organic cause).
Brain MRI (T1, T2, FLAIR) — recommended at first episode (NICE CG178).
Brain CT — when MRI is unavailable.
8.1 General principles (NICE CG178 · APA 2021 · WFSBP 2015 · Maudsley 14e consensus)
– pharmacotherapy + psychosocial intervention + systemic support. Pharmacotherapy alone is not sufficient for functional recovery.
(shared decision making) — medication, dose, and side effect profile are discussed; adherence begins at this stage.
Low-dose titration for initial episodes may be sufficient at half the standard dose.
Partial response in 2–4 weeks, full response at adequate dose expected in 4–6 weeks. If no response — medication is changed.
Antipsychotic polypharmacy increases side effect burden, evidence of effect weak; clozapine + second antipsychotic may be exception.
— Failure to respond to two different antipsychotics at adequate dose and duration (each 6–8 weeks)—clozapine initiated.

— At least 1–2 years after first episode, long-term/lifetime after multiple episodes. Early discontinuation of antipsychotic increases relapse risk 5-fold (Leucht S. et al. Lancet 2012, n=6493 meta-analysis).
CBTp and family intervention should be offered to all patients (NICE CG178 — strong recommendation).
(long-acting injectable) — in cases of adherence issues or patient preference; better outcome in relapse prevention after first episode (Tiihonen J. et al. JAMA Psychiatry 2017).
Mechanism, dose, evidence, and side effect profile for each medication.
High efficacy, but metabolic risk high
Widely used, inexpensive, LAI variant available.
Metabolic profile very good; must be taken with food

— haloperidol 5–20 mg/day, chlorpromazine 200–800 mg/day, flupentixol 6–18 mg/day, zuclopenthixol 25–150 mg/day. Effective for positive symptoms, but weak effect on EPS, tardive dyskinesia and negative symptoms—not superior when atypical antipsychotics available.

Primary mechanism of all antipsychotics is mesolimbic D2 receptor antagonism (in atypicals, additional 5-HT2A antagonism—contributes to negative symptoms and EPS reduction). 60–80% D2 occupancy is therapeutic range; >80% increases EPS risk.

— The single superior evidence-based medication in TRS (Kane J. et al. Arch Gen Psychiatry 1988 initial RCT; Siskind D. et al. Br J Psychiatry 2016 meta-analysis). 30–60% of TRS patients respond to clozapine.
Low D2 occupancy (40–60%), but strong effect on various receptors (D4, 5-HT2A, M1, α1); fewer EPS, better effect on negative symptoms; reduces suicide (FDA indication).

12.5 mg starting; gradually increase to 200–450 mg/day (max 900 mg/day). Complete blood count + leukocyte formula — weekly for first 18 weeks, then every 2 weeks for up to 1 year, then monthly. Neutrophil
Myocarditis — especially within the first 4 weeks; troponin, ECG.
Constipation — risk of ileus and perforation; prophylactic laxative.
Seizures — risk increases at doses > 600 mg/day; EEG monitoring.

(more severe than original symptoms), cholinergic rebound (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea) and adrenergic rebound (agitation, insomnia). If discontinuation is required due to agranulocytosis — patient is gradually switched to another antipsychotic (olanzapine or quetiapine) in an inpatient setting. For planned discontinuation — 25% reduction per week.
